skill.name: find-replace
skill.description: Use the sd command-line tool for fast find-and-replace work across files, including regex patterns, batch edits, and safe preview workflows.
skill.summary: Guide bulk text replacement with sd, ripgrep, fd, and review-first workflows.
skill.author: 0xDarkMatter

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell-command patterns because this skill documents Bash examples for sd, rg, fd, git, and Docker. Review found no prompt injection, network exfiltration, obfuscation, or confirmed malicious intent, but the skill can guide broad in-place file edits and includes command substitution and environment-file examples that require user caution.

medium_findings.title: Bash-Based Bulk File Replacement
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for operational risk, not malware. The skill explicitly uses Bash examples that run sd against files and globs, which can rewrite many project files if the user applies an incorrect pattern.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files clearly document in-place replacement commands and file globs. The context is legitimate documentation, but the commands can still cause broad file modifications.

medium_findings.title: Command Substitution For File Selection
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for shell safety risk. Several examples pass command output into sd, which can mishandle unusual filenames or expand the edit scope if the selection command returns unexpected paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command-substitution examples are visible and semantically tied to batch replacement. The risk is misuse and filename handling, not evidence of hostile intent.

medium_findings.title: Environment File Replacement Example
medium_findings.description: Verdict: NEEDS_REVIEW. The advanced guide shows replacing database connection variable names in environment files, which could alter secret-bearing configuration files even though it does not exfiltrate values.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines explicitly target .env-style files and database connection naming. There is no network or credential theft evidence, so the concern is controlled configuration editing.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Match Is A False Positive
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The analyzer appears to interpret the tool name sd as a weak cryptographic algorithm, but the file describes the sd text replacement utility.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding metadata identifies sd as a find-and-replace tool. No hashing, encryption, or security-sensitive cryptographic operation appears at this location.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Static Match Is A False Positive
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The referenced pattern is an import path replacement example, not file traversal logic or an attempt to access parent directories at runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The strings are examples for rewriting source import paths. They are not used to read, write, or escape a filesystem boundary by themselves.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Static Matches Are Benign Workflow Checks
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The git and search commands are used to list files, preview replacements, and review edits, which matches the skill purpose.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are presented as safety practices around bulk edits. No evidence found that they collect host information for exfiltration or persistence.

dangerous_patterns.title: In-Place Replacement Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples run sd directly against files and file globs. This is expected for the skill, but it can rewrite many files when used with broad patterns.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and central to the skill. The risk depends on user-selected patterns and file scopes.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples combine sd with command substitution from rg, git, and pwd. This can create unsafe argument lists if filenames contain spaces or unexpected characters.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is clearly present in multiple examples. The security impact is moderate because the commands are examples and not hidden automation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Docker Shell Invocation Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The advanced guide includes a Docker command that runs a shell command in a container. This is legitimate documentation, but it expands execution beyond local text replacement.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The Docker example is explicit and uses a shell inside a container. No evidence found of malicious image use or network exfiltration.

content.value_statement: Large codebases make manual text replacement slow and error-prone. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical sd workflows for previewing, applying, and reviewing batch replacements.
actual_capabilities: Explains basic sd commands for replacing text in one file or multiple files.
Shows regex patterns for capture groups, word boundaries, alternation, and multiline replacements.
Provides workflows that preview matches with ripgrep before applying replacements.
Demonstrates batch file selection with rg, fd, git, and shell loops.
Includes examples for imports, API paths, configuration values, React code, and cleanup tasks.
Documents escaping rules for common special characters in replacement patterns.
limitations: Requires the sd command-line tool to be installed separately.
Does not validate replacement patterns before commands are run.
Can modify many files if the user chooses a broad glob or pattern.
Does not provide an automated rollback beyond recommending git review.

use_cases.title: Rename Project Symbols
use_cases.description: Use word-boundary patterns to rename functions, variables, or identifiers across source files while reducing partial matches.

use_cases.title: Migrate Imports And Paths
use_cases.description: Convert relative imports, endpoint paths, or framework-specific attribute names during refactors and migrations.

use_cases.title: Clean Debug Code
use_cases.description: Preview and remove console logs, debug comments, or TODO markers before a release review.

prompt_templates.title: Preview A Simple Replacement
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner replacement in one project folder
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the find-replace skill to plan a safe sd command that replaces OLD_TEXT with NEW_TEXT in the src folder. Show the preview step first.

prompt_templates.title: Rename A Symbol Safely
prompt_templates.scenario: Avoid partial matches during a rename
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the find-replace skill to rename oldName to newName across TypeScript files. Use word boundaries and include verification steps.

prompt_templates.title: Update Imports Across Files
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the find-replace skill to migrate imports from a relative utils path to the alias path. Include pattern escaping and a git diff review step.

prompt_templates.title: Build A Batch Replacement Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the find-replace skill to design a cautious batch workflow for several replacements. Limit scope, preview each pattern, and explain rollback checks.

output_examples.input: A developer wants to rename a function across TypeScript files without changing similar names.
output: The assistant proposes a word-boundary replacement pattern.
It previews matches before editing and limits the scope to TypeScript files.
It finishes with a search check and a git diff review.

output_examples.input: A team needs to update API paths from version one to version two in source files.
output: The assistant identifies the exact path pattern and replacement text.
It recommends previewing affected files before applying the change.
It suggests reviewing changed files and testing endpoint references afterward.

output_examples.input: A release engineer wants to remove debug statements before shipping.
output: The assistant narrows the search to known debug patterns.
It recommends applying the change to a small file set first.
It calls out the need to review removed lines before committing.

best_practices: Preview every replacement with a read-only search before applying changes.
Keep file scopes narrow and expand only after confirming the pattern works.
Review the diff and run relevant tests after batch replacements.
anti_patterns: Running broad replacements across the repository without previewing matches.
Using unescaped regex characters when the intended match is literal text.
Editing environment files or generated files without an explicit backup or review plan.

faq.question: What tool does this skill use?
faq.answer: It uses sd, a command-line replacement tool designed as a simpler alternative to sed.

faq.question: Does the skill install sd automatically?
faq.answer: No. Users must install sd separately before running the documented commands.

faq.question: Can it preview changes before editing files?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill recommends previewing with ripgrep before applying replacements.

faq.question: Can it replace text across many files?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows file globs and file-selection workflows for batch replacements.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive configuration files?
faq.answer: Use caution. The skill includes environment-file examples, so users should preview and review those changes carefully.

faq.question: Does it include rollback support?
faq.answer: It does not automate rollback. It recommends using git and reviewing diffs before committing changes.
